title: Scope Management
summary: How to define, document and control exactly what the project will and will not deliver.

sections.heading: Product scope vs project scope
sections.body:
Product scope is the set of features and functions that characterise the deliverable, the thing the customer ends up with. Project scope is the work required to produce that deliverable, and it includes the management activities needed to deliver it.

Measuring completeness is the giveaway: product scope is judged against requirements, while project scope is judged against the scope baseline. An exam question that talks about features satisfied is pointing at product scope; one that talks about work completed is pointing at project scope.
sections.tip: Features delivered = product scope; work performed = project scope.

sections.heading: Collecting and defining requirements
sections.body:
Requirements turn stakeholder needs and expectations into documented, agreed and testable statements of what the solution must do. Elicit them through interviews, workshops, prototyping and other engagement techniques, then record them so each can be traced, prioritised and verified.

The requirements traceability matrix links every requirement back to its origin (the business need or stakeholder) and forward to the deliverable, test and acceptance criterion that satisfy it. This keeps the scope honest: nothing is built that no one asked for, and nothing requested is quietly dropped.
sections.tip: If a requirement cannot be tested or traced, it is not yet a requirement.

sections.heading: The scope baseline
sections.body:
The scope baseline is the approved version of three artefacts taken together: the project scope statement, the work breakdown structure (WBS), and the WBS dictionary. Once approved it can only change through formal change control.

The WBS itself, its decomposition rules and the 100 per cent rule are covered in the Schedule and Network guide, so refer there for the mechanics. For scope, the point to hold is that the baseline is the agreed yardstick against which all later scope decisions and performance are measured.
sections.tip: Scope baseline = scope statement + WBS + WBS dictionary, all approved.

sections.heading: Scope creep vs gold plating
sections.body:
Scope creep is scope growing in an uncontrolled way, with changes slipping in without analysis or approval. It is usually stakeholder-driven, the steady accretion of just one more small thing.

Gold plating is the delivery team adding extras the customer never requested, often to show off or to use up slack. It is team-driven.

Both are problems, not favours. Unrequested work consumes budget and schedule, adds risk and can void acceptance. The correct response to either is the same: protect the approved scope and route any genuine change through integrated change control so it is assessed and authorised before work begins.
sections.tip: Creep is stakeholder-driven, gold plating is team-driven; both are wrong, both go through change control.

sections.heading: Validate Scope vs Control Scope
sections.body:
Validate Scope is about formal acceptance: the customer or sponsor reviews completed deliverables and signs them off as accepted. Its output is accepted deliverables, and it is generally a customer-facing activity.

Control Scope is about monitoring the status of project and product scope and managing changes to the scope baseline. It is the internal discipline that prevents creep and keeps work aligned to the baseline.

The trap is mixing them up: validate scope = acceptance of deliverables, control scope = preventing and managing scope change. They often run in parallel but answer different questions.
sections.tip: Validate = is it accepted? Control = is scope still on baseline?

sections.heading: Prioritising requirements
sections.body:
Not all requirements carry equal weight, and prioritisation makes the trade-offs explicit. MoSCoW (Must have, Should have, Could have, Won't have this time) is a common technique for ranking them, especially when scope must flex against time or budget.

Detailed prioritisation and backlog techniques sit with adaptive delivery, so see the Agile and Hybrid Techniques guide rather than treating it here. For scope, simply know that agreed priorities steer what gets built first and what is dropped under pressure.
sections.tip: MoSCoW ranks requirements; see the Agile and Hybrid guide for the detail.

source: PM Cram study notes — reworded for the current ECO-based PMP exam.
